prompt: Which problem is the BEST fit for AI rather than deterministic code?
choices: Calculating sales tax from a known rate table
Routing a support ticket by an exact priority lookup table
Validating that an email address has a valid format
Summarizing the themes across 500 free-text customer reviews
explanation: Summarizing themes across unstructured free text is fuzzy, open-ended work where AI excels. The other options follow exact, known rules that deterministic code handles more cheaply and reliably.

prompt: Select ALL reasons that deterministic code is often preferable to an AI model when the rule is exact and known.
choices: It gives the same answer every time for the same input
Its behavior is predictable, so the result can be verified directly
It is non-deterministic, which is desirable for exact rules
It is typically cheaper and faster per execution and easier to test
explanation: For exact rules you want repeatable, cheap, verifiable behavior, which deterministic code provides. Non-determinism is the opposite of what an exact rule needs, so that option is wrong.

prompt: A PM/Product Owner is deciding how to source an AI feature that is NOT the product's differentiator, where the team has no labeled data and needs to ship quickly. Which sourcing choice best fits, and why?
choices: Build a model in-house, because owning the model always lowers total cost
Buy the most proprietary SaaS available, because lock-in reduces switching costs
Fine-tune a model immediately, because fine-tuning never requires labeled data
Call a hosted API behind an abstraction layer, because it ships fast and keeps vendor lock-in low while leaving other options open
explanation: For a non-differentiating feature with no data and tight timelines, a hosted API behind an abstraction layer ships fast and keeps options open. Building or fine-tuning needs data and time, and maximizing lock-in raises switching costs rather than lowering them.

prompt: Select ALL statements that correctly describe total cost of ownership (TCO) and vendor lock-in when sourcing an AI feature.
choices: The cheapest per-call price always means the lowest total cost of ownership
Vendor lock-in raises switching costs when proprietary formats and prompts make leaving expensive
TCO includes integration, evaluation, monitoring, and maintenance — not just the per-call price
An abstraction layer over the provider helps keep switching costs low
explanation: TCO spans integration, evaluation, monitoring, and maintenance, and an abstraction layer limits lock-in. The cheapest per-call price can still carry high TCO once those other costs are counted, so that option is wrong.

prompt: A capability is somewhat important but not your core differentiation, a solid hosted option exists, and your team is small. Which build-vs-buy *trade-off* fits?
choices: Build it because relying on a vendor is a risk you should never take
Wait and do nothing until you can afford to build it
Build it in-house because owning more of the stack is always better
Buy/integrate the hosted option now, and revisit building only if it later becomes true differentiation you can resource
explanation: When a capability is not your differentiator and a solid hosted option exists, buying now and revisiting later is the pragmatic trade-off for a small team. Building everything or doing nothing wastes scarce resources on non-core work.
